name: Dinosaurs & Paleontology
description: From T. rex to Triceratops — explore 40 species across 230 million years of prehistoric life. Identify dinosaurs from illustrations, learn about geological periods, and discover the scientists who unearthed them.

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur is famous for its enormous jaws, tiny arms, and banana-sized teeth?
facts.explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex was the apex predator of the Late Cretaceous, living 68-66 million years ago in western North America. Its teeth reached about 30cm and had a bite force exceeding 12,000 pounds. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Massive bipedal predator with enormous toothed skull, tiny vestigial forelimbs, and powerful legs, standing in a Late Cretaceous landscape

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Tyrannosaurus rex live?
facts.explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 68-66 million years ago, going extinct at the Cretaceous-Paleogene (K-Pg) mass extinction event. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
facts.visualDescription: T. rex silhouette against a Cretaceous sky with ferns and flowering plants below

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Tyrannosaurus rex?
facts.explanation: Tyrannosaurus rex reached approximately 12 meters in length, making it one of the largest land predators ever. It weighed around 8.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
facts.visualDescription: T. rex silhouette with a scale bar showing 12 meters in length beside a human figure for comparison

facts.quizQuestion: What is the name of the famous T. rex specimen on display at the Field Museum in Chicago?
facts.explanation: The T. rex specimen nicknamed 'Sue' (FMNH PR 2081) is one of the largest and most complete T. rex skeletons ever found. It is displayed at the Field Museum of Natural History in Chicago. Source: Wikipedia/Tyrannosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Enormous T. rex skeleton mounted in a grand museum hall, its massive skull at eye level with visitors below

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was the largest carnivorous dinosaur known, featuring a massive neural spine sail on its back?
facts.explanation: Spinosaurus was the largest known carnivorous dinosaur, reaching about 14 meters. It lived 99-93 million years ago in North Africa and had a distinctive sail formed by neural spines up to 1.65 meters tall. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Enormous crocodile-snouted dinosaur wading through a river delta, its massive dorsal sail rising above the water, catching North African sunlight

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Spinosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Spinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 99-93 million years ago, in what is now Morocco and Egypt in North Africa. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Spinosaurus in a lush Cretaceous North African river delta environment with mangrove-like trees

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Spinosaurus?
facts.explanation: Spinosaurus reached approximately 14 meters in length, making it longer than Tyrannosaurus rex. It weighed around 7.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Spinosaurus length compared to T. rex with scale markers, showing Spinosaurus extends noticeably further

facts.quizQuestion: What was the primary diet of Spinosaurus, unlike most large theropods?
facts.explanation: Spinosaurus was primarily a fish-eater (piscivore), evidenced by its narrow crocodile-like snout, conical teeth, and semi-aquatic adaptations. It is unlike most large theropods that hunted land animals. Source: Wikipedia/Spinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Spinosaurus lunging into a river to catch a large fish, its narrow jaws snatching prey from the water

facts.quizQuestion: Which Late Jurassic apex predator is the most common large carnivore found in the Morrison Formation of North America?
facts.explanation: Allosaurus was the apex predator of the Late Jurassic period, 155-150 million years ago, and is extremely abundant in the Morrison Formation of the western United States. It was named 'different lizard' by Othniel Charles Marsh. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Powerful three-clawed predator charging through a Late Jurassic landscape of conifers and ferns, massive skull low to the ground

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Allosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Allosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 155-150 million years ago. It coexisted with sauropods like Diplodocus and Brachiosaurus in the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Allosaurus in a Jurassic fern-filled forest landscape with giant cycads and conifers

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Allosaurus?
facts.explanation: Allosaurus reached approximately 8.5 meters in length and weighed around 2.3 tonnes. While smaller than T. rex, it was the dominant predator of its Jurassic ecosystem. Source: Wikipedia/Allosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Allosaurus skeleton mount showing its 8.5-meter length with three-clawed forelimbs prominent

facts.quizQuestion: Which small feathered dinosaur from Mongolia had a sickle-shaped killing claw on each foot?
facts.explanation: Velociraptor was a small feathered dinosaur from Mongolia, about 1.8 meters long and weighing only 15 kg. It had a distinctive curved sickle claw on its second toe. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
facts.visualDescription: Turkey-sized feathered dinosaur with a large curved sickle claw raised on its second toe, shown in the Mongolian Gobi Desert

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Velociraptor live?
facts.explanation: Velociraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 75-71 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia and China. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
facts.visualDescription: Velociraptor in a dry Cretaceous Mongolian Gobi Desert landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was actually turkey-sized in real life, despite being portrayed as human-sized in Jurassic Park?
facts.explanation: Real Velociraptors were about 1.8 meters long and weighed only 15 kg — roughly turkey-sized. The Jurassic Park 'raptors' were actually based on Deinonychus, a much larger relative at 3.4 meters. Source: Wikipedia/Velociraptor
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side size comparison of a real turkey-sized Velociraptor versus the human-sized Jurassic Park depiction

facts.quizQuestion: Which Late Jurassic theropod had a distinctive horn on its nose, giving it the name 'horned lizard'?
facts.explanation: Ceratosaurus was a Late Jurassic predator with a distinctive nasal horn, giving it its name meaning 'horned lizard'. It also had a row of osteoderms along its back and coexisted with Allosaurus in the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Medium-sized theropod with a prominent nasal horn and a row of osteoderms along its spine, stalking through Jurassic forest undergrowth

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Ceratosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Ceratosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 153-148 million years ago. It coexisted with Allosaurus and the giant sauropods of the Morrison Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Ceratosaurus and Allosaurus both present in a Late Jurassic Morrison Formation landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Ceratosaurus?
facts.explanation: Ceratosaurus was approximately 6 meters long and weighed around 0.5 tonnes. This made it smaller than its contemporary Allosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Ceratosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Ceratosaurus skeleton showing four-fingered hands and nasal horn, labeled with 6-meter scale bar

facts.quizQuestion: Which Argentinian theropod had bull-like horns above its eyes and even tinier arms than T. rex?
facts.explanation: Carnotaurus was a Late Cretaceous predator from Argentina with distinctive bull-like horns above its eyes. It had the most vestigial forelimbs of any large theropod — even more reduced than T. rex's arms. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
facts.visualDescription: Muscular predator with two prominent bull-like horns above its eyes charging forward, its tiny stub-like forelimbs useless against its powerful body

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Carnotaurus live?
facts.explanation: Carnotaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 72-69 million years ago, in what is now Argentina. It was a member of the Abelisauridae family. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
facts.visualDescription: Carnotaurus silhouette against a Late Cretaceous Patagonian landscape of sparse conifers

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Carnotaurus?
facts.explanation: Carnotaurus was approximately 8 meters long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Carnotaurus
facts.visualDescription: Carnotaurus running at full sprint, body low and horizontal, horned head forward, tiny arms folded against its sides

facts.quizQuestion: Which Early Jurassic theropod had a distinctive double crest on its skull, giving it the name 'two-crested lizard'?
facts.explanation: Dilophosaurus had two parallel crests running along its skull, giving it the name 'two-crested lizard'. It was one of the earliest large predatory dinosaurs, living about 193 million years ago in what is now Arizona. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Graceful Early Jurassic predator with two parallel crests along its skull, no frill or venom sac in sight, stalking through an Arizona desert landscape

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Dilophosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Dilophosaurus lived during the Early Jurassic period, approximately 193 million years ago. It was one of the earliest large predatory dinosaurs, found in Arizona and China. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Dilophosaurus in an Early Jurassic Arizona desert with sparse vegetation and red rock formations

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was incorrectly portrayed as having a neck frill and venomous spit in Jurassic Park, features invented by the author?
facts.explanation: Dilophosaurus was portrayed in Jurassic Park with a foldable neck frill and venom-spitting ability. These features have no basis in the fossil record and were invented by Michael Crichton. The real Dilophosaurus was simply a crested predator. Source: Wikipedia/Dilophosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Real Dilophosaurus fossil skull with twin crests but no frill socket, contrasted with a fictional Jurassic Park-style depiction struck through with an X

facts.quizQuestion: Which chicken-sized dinosaur found in Germany is one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs?
facts.explanation: Compsognathus was approximately 65 cm long and weighed about 3.5 kg — roughly chicken-sized. It was found in the Solnhofen limestone of Germany, the same deposits that preserved Archaeopteryx. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
facts.visualDescription: Tiny agile dinosaur roughly the size of a chicken, darting through a Late Jurassic European island environment with shallow lagoons

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Compsognathus live?
facts.explanation: Compsognathus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 150 million years ago, in what are now Germany and France. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
facts.visualDescription: Compsognathus on a Late Jurassic European limestone island shore with an Archaeopteryx perched nearby in a tree

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Compsognathus?
facts.explanation: Compsognathus was approximately 0.65 meters long (65 cm) — roughly the size of a chicken. Source: Wikipedia/Compsognathus
facts.visualDescription: Tiny Compsognathus with a scale bar showing 65 cm, a chicken shown beside it at the same scale for comparison

facts.quizQuestion: Which Early Cretaceous dromaeosaurid, named 'terrible claw', was the actual inspiration for the Jurassic Park Velociraptors?
facts.explanation: Deinonychus was the real-world basis for the Jurassic Park 'Velociraptors'. At 3.4 meters long and 73 kg, it was far larger than Velociraptor. John Ostrom's 1969 description of Deinonychus revolutionized paleontology by suggesting dinosaurs were active and warm-blooded. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
facts.visualDescription: Large feathered dromaeosaurid with an enormous sickle claw raised on its second toe, hunting in Early Cretaceous North American woodland

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Deinonychus live?
facts.explanation: Deinonychus lived during the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 115-108 million years ago, in what is now Montana, Wyoming, and Oklahoma in the United States. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
facts.visualDescription: Deinonychus in an Early Cretaceous forest clearing in what is now Montana

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Deinonychus?
facts.explanation: Deinonychus was approximately 3.4 meters long and weighed about 73 kg. This is significantly larger than Velociraptor (1.8 m, 15 kg), which is why it was used as the basis for the Jurassic Park raptors. Source: Wikipedia/Deinonychus
facts.visualDescription: Deinonychus at 3.4 meters shown beside a 1.8-meter Velociraptor for size comparison, both feathered

facts.quizQuestion: Which South American theropod rivalled T. rex in size and likely hunted giant sauropods like Argentinosaurus?
facts.explanation: Giganotosaurus was a massive predator from Patagonia, Argentina, approximately 12.5 meters long. Named in 1995, it rivals T. rex in size and likely preyed on giant sauropods like Argentinosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Massive elongated-skulled predator stalking through Patagonian Late Cretaceous plains, a giant Argentinosaurus visible in the background

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Giganotosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Giganotosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 98-97 million years ago, in what is now Patagonia, Argentina. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Giganotosaurus silhouette against a Cretaceous Patagonian landscape of open plains

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Giganotosaurus?
facts.explanation: Giganotosaurus was approximately 12.5 meters long and weighed around 6.5 tonnes. Its elongated skull with blade-like serrated teeth distinguished it from T. rex. Source: Wikipedia/Giganotosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Giganotosaurus skull compared to T. rex skull, showing Giganotosaurus's slender elongated snout versus T. rex's deep robust skull

facts.quizQuestion: Which North African theropod's name means 'shark-toothed lizard', after the serrated blade-like teeth similar to a great white shark?
facts.explanation: Carcharodontosaurus gets its name from 'carcharias' (shark) and 'odontos' (tooth), referring to its serrated, blade-like teeth. It lived in North Africa 99-94 million years ago alongside Spinosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Massive theropod with serrated blade-like teeth in an open-jawed threat display, North African Cretaceous landscape behind it

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Carcharodontosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Carcharodontosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 99-94 million years ago, in what are now Morocco and Algeria in North Africa. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Carcharodontosaurus in a Cretaceous North African landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Carcharodontosaurus?
facts.explanation: Carcharodontosaurus reached approximately 12.5 meters in length and weighed around 6.2 tonnes, making it one of the largest carnivorous dinosaurs ever known. Source: Wikipedia/Carcharodontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Three massive theropod silhouettes in a row — Spinosaurus, Carcharodontosaurus, and T. rex — with scale bars showing their near-identical lengths

facts.quizQuestion: Which tyrannosaurid dinosaur is named after the Canadian province where a bonebed of 26 individuals was discovered?
facts.explanation: Albertosaurus is named after Alberta, Canada, where Barnum Brown discovered a bonebed containing 26 individuals in 1910. This is considered evidence of pack behavior. It was a smaller relative of T. rex. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Lean tyrannosaur with a narrower skull than T. rex, moving through a Late Cretaceous Alberta forest of conifers and flowering plants

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Albertosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Albertosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70-68 million years ago, in what is now Alberta, Canada. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Albertosaurus in a Late Cretaceous Alberta conifer forest

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Albertosaurus?
facts.explanation: Albertosaurus was approximately 9 meters long and weighed around 1.3 tonnes — significantly smaller than its relative T. rex at 12 meters and 8.4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Albertosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Albertosaurus skeleton showing its slimmer, longer proportions compared to T. rex outline

facts.quizQuestion: Which fish-eating spinosaurid discovered in Surrey, England has a name meaning 'heavy claw' from its enormous curved thumb claw?
facts.explanation: Baryonyx means 'heavy claw', named for its large curved thumb claw. It was discovered in Surrey, England in 1983 and had a crocodile-like skull adapted for eating fish. Fish scales were found in its stomach contents. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
facts.visualDescription: Spinosaurid with a long narrow crocodile-like snout holding a large fish, its enormous curved thumb claw raised, in an Early Cretaceous English wetland

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Baryonyx live?
facts.explanation: Baryonyx lived during the Early Cretaceous period, approximately 130-125 million years ago, in England, Spain, and Portugal. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
facts.visualDescription: Baryonyx wading in an Early Cretaceous English wetland, surrounded by ferns and early flowering plants

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Baryonyx?
facts.explanation: Baryonyx was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 1.7 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Baryonyx
facts.visualDescription: Baryonyx scale comparison in a modern British setting showing its 10-meter length

facts.quizQuestion: Which slender early dinosaur from the Late Triassic has its name meaning 'hollow form', from its hollow bones?
facts.explanation: Coelophysis means 'hollow form', referring to its hollow bones. It is one of the earliest known dinosaurs, living approximately 213-203 million years ago. A spectacular mass death assemblage of hundreds of skeletons was found at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
facts.visualDescription: Slender agile early theropod with hollow bones, dozens of them visible fossilized in a bonebed at Ghost Ranch, New Mexico

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Coelophysis live?
facts.explanation: Coelophysis lived during the Late Triassic period, approximately 213-203 million years ago — making it one of the earliest known dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
facts.visualDescription: Coelophysis in a Late Triassic landscape with primitive conifers and ferns, before flowering plants existed

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Coelophysis?
facts.explanation: Coelophysis was approximately 3 meters long and weighed about 25 kg. Despite being slender and small by later theropod standards, it was a fast and effective predator. Source: Wikipedia/Coelophysis
facts.visualDescription: Slender Coelophysis skeleton showing its hollow-boned, gracile build at 3 meters length

facts.quizQuestion: Which fast-running omnivorous theropod from Mongolia has a name meaning 'chicken mimic' and was featured in a herd scene in Jurassic Park?
facts.explanation: Gallimimus means 'chicken mimic', from its beak and overall body plan resembling a chicken scaled up enormously. It was an ornithomimid ('ostrich mimic' family) that could run at an estimated 40-70 km/h. It appeared in Jurassic Park's famous herd stampede scene. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
facts.visualDescription: Ostrich-like dinosaur with long legs and a small toothless beak running at full sprint across a Mongolian plain

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Gallimimus live?
facts.explanation: Gallimimus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70 million years ago, in what is now the Nemegt Formation of Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
facts.visualDescription: Gallimimus herd running across an open Late Cretaceous Mongolian steppe

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Gallimimus?
facts.explanation: Gallimimus was approximately 6 meters long and weighed about 440 kg. Despite this size, it was toothless and likely omnivorous or herbivorous. Source: Wikipedia/Gallimimus
facts.visualDescription: Gallimimus shown at 6 meters length alongside a human figure and an ostrich for scale comparison

facts.quizQuestion: Which bizarre herbivorous theropod from Mongolia had the longest claws of any known animal ever — up to 1 meter long?
facts.explanation: Therizinosaurus had claws estimated up to 1 meter long — the longest claws of any known animal in Earth's history. Despite being a theropod in the same group as T. rex, it was entirely herbivorous with a pot belly. Its name means 'scythe lizard'. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Enormous pot-bellied theropod with three massive scythe-like claws over a meter long, calmly pulling tree branches down to eat in a Mongolian forest

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Therizinosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Therizinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 70-68 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Therizinosaurus in a Late Cretaceous Mongolian forest, its massive scythe claws clearly visible

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Therizinosaurus?
facts.explanation: Therizinosaurus was approximately 10 meters long and weighed around 5 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Therizinosaurus scale comparison showing its 10-meter length and massive pot-bellied build

facts.quizQuestion: Despite being a theropod in the same group as T. rex, what was Therizinosaurus's diet?
facts.explanation: Therizinosaurus was herbivorous — it ate plants despite being a theropod (the same clade as all large meat-eating dinosaurs). It used its enormous claws to pull down branches and its pot belly housed a large digestive system for plant matter. Source: Wikipedia/Therizinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Therizinosaurus pot belly visible as it pulls leafy branches down, clearly showing its herbivorous nature despite its terrifying claws

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur's name means 'egg thief', though it was later found to be brooding its own eggs rather than stealing them?
facts.explanation: Oviraptor means 'egg thief' — it was originally thought to be stealing Protoceratops eggs when first discovered by Roy Chapman Andrews in 1923. Later fossil evidence showed it was actually brooding its own eggs, completely vindicating the animal. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
facts.visualDescription: Oviraptor crouched protectively over a clutch of eggs in a brooding pose, its parrot-like beak and head crest visible

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Oviraptor live?
facts.explanation: Oviraptor lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 75-71 million years ago, in what is now Mongolia. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
facts.visualDescription: Oviraptor in a Late Cretaceous Mongolian desert landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many meters long was Oviraptor?
facts.explanation: Oviraptor was approximately 1.5 meters long and weighed about 35 kg — roughly the size of a large dog. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
facts.visualDescription: Oviraptor shown at 1.5 meters length beside a large dog for scale comparison

facts.quizQuestion: Which American paleontologist-explorer led the expedition that first discovered Oviraptor in Mongolia in 1923?
facts.explanation: Roy Chapman Andrews led the Central Asiatic Expeditions of the American Museum of Natural History in the 1920s. He discovered Oviraptor in Mongolia in 1923 along with many other significant fossil finds. Some say he inspired the Indiana Jones character. Source: Wikipedia/Oviraptor
facts.visualDescription: Classic 1920s explorer-paleontologist excavating fossils in a Mongolian desert, wide-brimmed hat against the harsh sun

facts.quizQuestion: Which long-necked sauropod had pencil-like teeth used for stripping leaves and a whip-like tail?
facts.explanation: Diplodocus had slender, peg-like teeth designed for raking leaves off branches rather than chewing. Its tail was extremely long and whip-like, possibly capable of producing a supersonic crack. Source: Wikipedia/Diplodocus
facts.visualDescription: Enormously long dinosaur with a gracefully arching neck and a tapering whip-like tail, grazing on tall cycads in a lush Jurassic landscape

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Diplodocus live?
facts.explanation: Diplodocus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 154–152 million years ago, in what is now the Morrison Formation of the western United States. Source: Wikipedia/Diplodocus
facts.visualDescription: Diplodocus silhouette against a warm Late Jurassic sky with towering conifers and cycads

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many metres long was Diplodocus?
facts.explanation: Diplodocus reached approximately 26 metres in length, making it one of the longest dinosaurs known, though much of that length was neck and tail. Source: Wikipedia/Diplodocus
facts.visualDescription: Overhead scale diagram of a 26-metre Diplodocus next to a double-decker bus for comparison

facts.quizQuestion: Which sauropod's whip-like tail may have been able to produce a supersonic crack?
facts.explanation: Paleontologists have proposed that Diplodocus could whip its long tail fast enough to break the sound barrier, producing a sonic boom — similar to cracking a whip. The name 'Diplodocus' means 'double beam', referring to a distinctive tail bone structure. Source: Wikipedia/Diplodocus
facts.visualDescription: Diplodocus swinging its long tapering tail dramatically, motion lines showing a crack at the tip like a whip

facts.quizQuestion: Which sauropod had front legs longer than its hind legs, giving it a giraffe-like posture?
facts.explanation: Brachiosaurus is unusual among sauropods because its front limbs were longer than its hind limbs, holding its neck at a steep upward angle like a giraffe. Its name literally means 'arm lizard'. Source: Wikipedia/Brachiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Tall sauropod with an exceptionally upright neck angle, front legs visibly longer than hind legs, reaching up into a tall tree canopy

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Brachiosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Brachiosaurus lived during the Late Jurassic period, approximately 154–153 million years ago. It is known from the Morrison Formation in the United States and from the Tendaguru Formation in Tanzania. Source: Wikipedia/Brachiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Brachiosaurus towering above lush Jurassic vegetation under a warm hazy sky

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many tonnes did Brachiosaurus weigh?
facts.explanation: Brachiosaurus is estimated to have weighed approximately 56 tonnes, making it one of the heaviest dinosaurs known from reasonably complete material. Source: Wikipedia/Brachiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Scale comparison of a Brachiosaurus beside an African elephant, the elephant dwarfed by the massive dinosaur

facts.quizQuestion: Which sauropod had nostrils positioned high on its skull and was famously depicted in the opening scene of Jurassic Park?
facts.explanation: Brachiosaurus had its nasal openings situated high on the top of its skull. It is the sauropod featured in the memorable first dinosaur reveal scene of the 1993 film Jurassic Park, where characters see it feeding in a tree. Source: Wikipedia/Brachiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Majestic sauropod standing tall, its nostrils visible near the top of its skull, echoing the iconic Jurassic Park movie shot

facts.quizQuestion: Which sauropod was long known by the nickname 'Thunder Lizard' and had its genus name debated for over a century?
facts.explanation: Apatosaurus was long called Brontosaurus ('thunder lizard') — but that name was considered invalid from 1903 until 2015, when a major study restored Brontosaurus as a valid separate genus. Both names are now accepted. Apatosaurus means 'deceptive lizard'. Source: Wikipedia/Apatosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Large sauropod dinosaur mid-stride across a Morrison Formation floodplain, its massive body silhouetted against a red sunset sky

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Apatosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Apatosaurus lived approximately 152–151 million years ago during the Late Jurassic period, in the Morrison Formation of the western United States alongside Diplodocus, Allosaurus, and Stegosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Apatosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Apatosaurus herd in a lush Jurassic forest clearing, large conifers towering alongside them

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was originally described as 'Brontosaurus', a name dismissed as invalid in 1903 but officially reinstated as a separate valid genus in 2015?
facts.explanation: The Brontosaurus/Apatosaurus naming controversy lasted over a century. In 1903 Elmer Riggs concluded Brontosaurus was just a species of Apatosaurus. A detailed 2015 study by Tschopp et al. found enough differences to restore Brontosaurus as a valid genus separate from Apatosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Apatosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Split image showing two similar sauropod skulls side by side with scientific name labels, representing the century-long taxonomy debate

facts.quizQuestion: Which titanosaur from Patagonia is considered one of the largest land animals ever, estimated at up to 70–80 tonnes?
facts.explanation: Argentinosaurus is estimated to have weighed 70–80 tonnes or more, making it a strong candidate for the largest land animal ever. It is known from partial remains including enormous vertebrae and limb bones found in Patagonia, Argentina. Source: Wikipedia/Argentinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Titanic sauropod dwarfing the Patagonian landscape, its massive vertebrae visible, scale figure of a human near its foot

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Argentinosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Argentinosaurus lived during the Late Cretaceous period, approximately 96–92 million years ago. It was a titanosaur — the most successful group of sauropods in the Cretaceous. Source: Wikipedia/Argentinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Argentinosaurus silhouette against a Cretaceous sky with distant volcanoes and dense subtropical vegetation

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many metres long was Argentinosaurus?
facts.explanation: Argentinosaurus is estimated to have reached approximately 35 metres in length, making it one of the longest land animals ever. Its enormous scale is known primarily from fragmentary vertebrae and limb bones. Source: Wikipedia/Argentinosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Top-down scale diagram showing a 35-metre Argentinosaurus compared to a basketball court

facts.quizQuestion: Which ceratopsian dinosaur had three facial horns and a large bony frill, and was a contemporary of Tyrannosaurus rex?
facts.explanation: Triceratops had two long brow horns and a shorter nose horn, plus a large bony frill extending behind its head. It lived at exactly the same time and place as T. rex during the latest Cretaceous, and fossil evidence shows the two animals sometimes fought. Source: Wikipedia/Triceratops
facts.visualDescription: Powerful ceratopsian charging forward, three prominent horns gleaming, its large bony frill flushed with vivid colour patterns

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Triceratops live?
facts.explanation: Triceratops lived 68–66 million years ago at the very end of the Late Cretaceous period, making it one of the last non-avian dinosaurs to exist before the mass extinction event. Source: Wikipedia/Triceratops
facts.visualDescription: Triceratops in a Late Cretaceous lowland environment with flowering plants and ferns, an asteroid trail faintly visible in the distant sky

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many metres long was Triceratops?
facts.explanation: Triceratops reached approximately 9 metres in length and weighed around 8 tonnes, making it one of the largest ceratopsian dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Triceratops
facts.visualDescription: Side profile of a 9-metre Triceratops beside a pickup truck for scale, its three horns clearly visible

facts.quizQuestion: Which horned dinosaur has fossil evidence showing combat scars inflicted by Tyrannosaurus rex?
facts.explanation: Fossil bones of Triceratops show healed bite marks and gouges consistent with Tyrannosaurus teeth, confirming the two species genuinely fought. Triceratops was named by Othniel Charles Marsh in 1889. It was among the very last non-avian dinosaurs, going extinct at the K-Pg boundary 66 million years ago. Source: Wikipedia/Triceratops
facts.visualDescription: Dramatic face-off between a Triceratops and a T. rex in a Late Cretaceous clearing, the ceratopsian lowering its three horns in defense

facts.quizQuestion: Which small ceratopsian from Mongolia is famous for a fossil showing it locked in combat with a Velociraptor, both buried alive by a sand dune?
facts.explanation: The 'fighting dinosaurs' fossil — a single specimen of Protoceratops and Velociraptor frozen in mortal combat — was found in the Gobi Desert and is one of the most dramatic fossils ever discovered. It was found by a Polish-Mongolian expedition in 1971. Source: Wikipedia/Protoceratops
facts.visualDescription: Two dinosaurs locked together in combat — a small frilled ceratopsian grappling with a feathered raptor — preserved in stone exactly as they died

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Protoceratops live?
facts.explanation: Protoceratops lived approximately 75–71 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous, in the Gobi Desert region of present-day Mongolia and China. It was discovered by Roy Chapman Andrews in 1922. Source: Wikipedia/Protoceratops
facts.visualDescription: Small frilled ceratopsian dinosaur scurrying across a Gobi Desert sand dune landscape under a blazing orange Cretaceous sky

facts.quizQuestion: Which ceratopsian dinosaur is thought to have inspired ancient Scythian griffin legends, as nomads found its beaked skulls in gold-bearing Gobi rocks?
facts.explanation: Folklorist Adrienne Mayor proposed that ancient Scythian nomads found Protoceratops skulls eroding from gold-bearing sediments in the Gobi Desert, inspiring the griffin legend — a creature with a bird's beak and four legs guarding gold. The connection is culturally compelling though unproven. Source: Wikipedia/Protoceratops
facts.visualDescription: Side-by-side of a Protoceratops skull and a classical Greek griffin carving, both sharing a beaked profile and quadrupedal form

facts.quizQuestion: Which ceratopsian had a single large nose horn and a spectacular frill edged with multiple long radiating spikes?
facts.explanation: Styracosaurus (meaning 'spiked lizard') had a prominent nasal horn and a frill adorned with several long spikes projecting outward from the edges — one of the most elaborate frill ornamentations of any ceratopsian. It was named by Lawrence Lambe in 1913. Source: Wikipedia/Styracosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Dramatic view of a Styracosaurus facing the viewer, its frill's long radiating spikes spread wide, the single nasal horn prominent

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Styracosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Styracosaurus lived approximately 75 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period, in what is now Alberta, Canada and Montana, USA. Source: Wikipedia/Styracosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Styracosaurus herd moving through a Cretaceous floodplain, their elaborate frills visible above the tall vegetation

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many metres long was Styracosaurus?
facts.explanation: Styracosaurus reached approximately 5.5 metres in length and weighed around 2.7 tonnes. Although shorter than Triceratops, its elaborate frill ornamentation was more spectacular. Source: Wikipedia/Styracosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Scale profile of a 5.5-metre Styracosaurus beside an average car, the frill spikes nearly as tall as the vehicle

facts.quizQuestion: Which Jurassic dinosaur had two rows of distinctive back plates and four tail spikes nicknamed the 'thagomizer'?
facts.explanation: Stegosaurus is immediately recognizable by its double row of kite-shaped back plates and the cluster of four tail spikes used for defense. The tail spike cluster was dubbed the 'thagomizer' in a Gary Larson Far Side comic strip — a name later officially adopted by paleontologists. Source: Wikipedia/Stegosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Iconic plated dinosaur in profile, its double row of large diamond-shaped back plates gleaming, the four tail spikes clearly visible

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Stegosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Stegosaurus lived approximately 155–150 million years ago during the Late Jurassic period, sharing its Morrison Formation habitat with Allosaurus, Diplodocus, and Apatosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Stegosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Stegosaurus grazing on low ferns in a Late Jurassic landscape alongside cycads and tall conifers

facts.quizQuestion: How many back plates did Stegosaurus have in total?
facts.explanation: Stegosaurus had 17 dermal plates arranged in two alternating rows along its back. The exact function of the plates — thermoregulation, display, or species recognition — is still debated by paleontologists. Source: Wikipedia/Stegosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Top-down view of a Stegosaurus showing all 17 back plates arranged in two alternating rows from neck to tail

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur's tail spike cluster was named 'thagomizer' after a Gary Larson Far Side cartoon — a term later officially adopted by paleontologists?
facts.explanation: In a 1982 Far Side comic, Gary Larson depicted cavemen being warned about the tail spikes of a dinosaur, calling them the 'thagomizer'. Paleontologists found the name so useful they officially adopted it into scientific literature. Source: Wikipedia/Stegosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Close-up of a Stegosaurus tail showing the four sharp spikes labelled with a humorous Far Side-style chalkboard sign reading 'Thagomizer'

facts.quizQuestion: Which armored dinosaur had a massive bony tail club capable of shattering predator bones, and was so armored that even its eyelids were protected?
facts.explanation: Ankylosaurus is often called a 'living tank'. It had extensive body armor of fused bony plates (osteoderms) embedded in its skin, and a massive club of ankylosed bone at the end of its tail. Even the sclerotic rings protecting its eyes were bony. Source: Wikipedia/Ankylosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Squat, tank-like dinosaur completely covered in bony armor plates, its thick tail club raised defensively, facing a threat head-on

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Ankylosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Ankylosaurus lived 68–66 million years ago during the final phase of the Late Cretaceous, making it one of the very last non-avian dinosaurs. It shared its world with Tyrannosaurus rex and Triceratops. Source: Wikipedia/Ankylosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Ankylosaurus browsing low vegetation in a Late Cretaceous forest alongside a distant herd of Triceratops

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was so thoroughly armored that bony plates were even embedded in its eyelids?
facts.explanation: Ankylosaurus had osteoderms (bony skin armor) covering its entire body including its eyelids, leaving virtually no soft spot for predators to exploit. The only vulnerable area was its relatively unprotected underside. Source: Wikipedia/Ankylosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Extreme close-up of an Ankylosaurus eye showing the bony armored eyelid clearly defined against the surrounding plated skin

facts.quizQuestion: Which bipedal herbivore had a skull dome up to 25 centimetres thick and a name meaning 'thick-headed lizard'?
facts.explanation: Pachycephalosaurus had an extraordinarily thick bony dome on top of its skull — up to 25 cm thick. This gives it its name ('pachys' = thick, 'kephale' = head). Whether it used this dome for head-butting rivals or for flank-butting or display is still debated. Source: Wikipedia/Pachycephalosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Bipedal dinosaur with a prominent smooth domed skull, lowering its head as if preparing to charge a rival

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Pachycephalosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Pachycephalosaurus lived 70–66 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous, in what is now Montana, Wyoming, and South Dakota. It is known almost entirely from skull material. Source: Wikipedia/Pachycephalosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Pachycephalosaurus in a Late Cretaceous highland setting, its domed skull silhouetted against a pale sky

facts.quizQuestion: How many centimetres thick was the skull dome of Pachycephalosaurus?
facts.explanation: The bony dome of Pachycephalosaurus could reach up to 25 centimetres in thickness — roughly the same thickness as a cinder block — making it the thickest skull structure of any known dinosaur. Source: Wikipedia/Pachycephalosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Cross-section diagram of a Pachycephalosaurus skull showing the 25 cm thick solid bone dome with a ruler for scale

facts.quizQuestion: Which duck-billed dinosaur had a long curved hollow crest that functioned as a resonating chamber to produce low-frequency calls?
facts.explanation: Parasaurolophus had a distinctive backward-curving hollow crest up to 1.8 metres long. CT scans of the crest have revealed internal passages that would have allowed it to produce deep, resonant, trumpet-like sounds for communication. Source: Wikipedia/Parasaurolophus
facts.visualDescription: Elegant duck-billed dinosaur in profile displaying its long curved hollow head crest, mouth open producing a visible sound wave

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Parasaurolophus live?
facts.explanation: Parasaurolophus lived approximately 76–74 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period, in what is now Alberta, Canada and New Mexico, USA. Source: Wikipedia/Parasaurolophus
facts.visualDescription: Parasaurolophus herd moving through a Late Cretaceous floodplain, their distinctive crests visible above the vegetation

facts.quizQuestion: How many metres long could the hollow head crest of Parasaurolophus grow?
facts.explanation: The backward-curving hollow crest of Parasaurolophus could reach up to approximately 1.8 metres in length in adult specimens. CT scanning confirmed the internal passages functioned as a resonating chamber. Source: Wikipedia/Parasaurolophus
facts.visualDescription: Life-size diagram of a Parasaurolophus skull with the 1.8-metre crest measured against a tape measure

facts.quizQuestion: Which crested hadrosaur could walk on either two or four legs depending on speed and terrain?
facts.explanation: Like many hadrosaurs, Parasaurolophus was facultatively bipedal — it could walk on all fours while browsing slowly and shift to a bipedal gait when moving faster. This flexibility gave it an advantage in varied Cretaceous habitats. Source: Wikipedia/Parasaurolophus
facts.visualDescription: Two-panel illustration: Parasaurolophus on all fours browsing low plants, then the same animal running on two legs with crest held back

facts.quizQuestion: Which Early Cretaceous ornithopod was the second dinosaur ever formally named (1825) and famously has a distinctive thumb spike?
facts.explanation: Iguanodon was formally named by Gideon Mantell in 1825, making it only the second dinosaur to receive a scientific name. Its distinctive conical thumb spike was initially misidentified and placed on the nose — later correctly identified on the hand. Source: Wikipedia/Iguanodon
facts.visualDescription: Large bipedal dinosaur showing its distinctive thumb spike prominently on its right hand, browsing cycads in an Early Cretaceous forest

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Iguanodon live?
facts.explanation: Iguanodon lived approximately 126–113 million years ago during the Early Cretaceous period, in what is now Belgium, England, Germany, and Spain. Source: Wikipedia/Iguanodon
facts.visualDescription: Iguanodon herd on an Early Cretaceous European landscape, broad flat-headed silhouettes against a coastal backdrop

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was found in extraordinary numbers in a Belgian coal mine in 1878, yielding 29 complete articulated skeletons in a single discovery?
facts.explanation: In 1878, coal miners in Bernissart, Belgium discovered 29 complete and articulated Iguanodon skeletons in a coal mine — one of the most significant dinosaur finds in history. The specimens are now displayed at the Royal Belgian Institute of Natural Sciences. Source: Wikipedia/Iguanodon
facts.visualDescription: Dramatic underground excavation scene in a dark Belgian coal mine, multiple complete dinosaur skeletons emerging from the rock walls

facts.quizQuestion: Which large duck-billed hadrosaur is known from mummified specimens with preserved skin, and has T. rex bite marks on its tail bones?
facts.explanation: Edmontosaurus is known from exceptionally preserved 'dinosaur mummies' — specimens with dried skin and even some soft tissue preserved. Fossilized tail vertebrae of Edmontosaurus have been found with healed T. rex bite marks, proving it was prey for the great predator. Source: Wikipedia/Edmontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Duck-billed hadrosaur in profile showing preserved skin texture detail, a scar visible on the tail where an old T. rex bite has healed over

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Edmontosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Edmontosaurus lived approximately 73–66 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous, surviving right up to the K-Pg mass extinction. It is named after Edmonton, Alberta. Source: Wikipedia/Edmontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Large hadrosaur herd grazing in a Late Cretaceous landscape, the horizon showing the distant smoke of volcanic activity

facts.quizQuestion: Approximately how many metres long was Edmontosaurus?
facts.explanation: Edmontosaurus reached approximately 13 metres in length, making it one of the largest hadrosaurs. It weighed around 4 tonnes. Source: Wikipedia/Edmontosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Scale comparison of a 13-metre Edmontosaurus beside a school bus, the dinosaur notably longer

facts.quizQuestion: Which hadrosaur's name means 'good mother lizard' and was the first dinosaur proven to care for its hatchlings in a nest?
facts.explanation: Maiasaura was named by Jack Horner and Robert Makela in 1979 after discovering nesting colonies in the Two Medicine Formation, Montana, with eggs, hatchlings, and juveniles together. This proved for the first time that at least some dinosaurs were attentive parents. Source: Wikipedia/Maiasaura
facts.visualDescription: Hadrosaur parent tending a nest full of eggs on a Montana hillside colony, hatchlings visible in nearby nests

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Maiasaura live?
facts.explanation: Maiasaura lived approximately 76.7 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period, in what is now the Two Medicine Formation of Montana, USA. Source: Wikipedia/Maiasaura
facts.visualDescription: Maiasaura herd crossing a Late Cretaceous Montana landscape under a dramatic sunset sky

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur is the only one to have had its remains sent to the International Space Station?
facts.explanation: A Maiasaura bone fragment and eggshell were carried aboard Space Shuttle mission STS-51-F in 1985 by astronaut Loren Acton, and later were present on the International Space Station — making Maiasaura the first dinosaur in space. Source: Wikipedia/Maiasaura
facts.visualDescription: Dinosaur bone fragment floating in zero gravity inside a Space Station module, the Earth visible through the window behind it

facts.quizQuestion: Which hadrosaur had a helmet-shaped hollow crest used for sound production and was named 'helmet lizard' after its corinthian-helmet-like head?
facts.explanation: Corythosaurus had a large, laterally compressed, helmet-like hollow crest resembling a Corinthian battle helmet — hence its name ('helmet lizard'). Like Parasaurolophus, the crest served as a resonating chamber for communication and possibly species recognition. Source: Wikipedia/Corythosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Duck-billed dinosaur with a tall rounded helmet-shaped crest rising from its skull, its elegant profile resembling a classical Greek warrior's helmet

facts.quizQuestion: In which geological period did Corythosaurus live?
facts.explanation: Corythosaurus lived approximately 77–75 million years ago during the Late Cretaceous period, in what is now Alberta, Canada. It was discovered by Barnum Brown in 1914. Source: Wikipedia/Corythosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Corythosaurus herd wading through a shallow Late Cretaceous river delta, their helmet crests reflected in the still water

facts.quizQuestion: Which crested hadrosaur is known from well-preserved skin impressions and was discovered by Barnum Brown in 1914 in Alberta?
facts.explanation: Corythosaurus specimens include several with preserved skin impressions, revealing a pebbly texture across much of the body. The species was first described by Barnum Brown in 1914 from Alberta's Dinosaur Park Formation. Source: Wikipedia/Corythosaurus
facts.visualDescription: Museum fossil mount of a Corythosaurus with inset showing a close-up of the pebbly skin impression texture preserved in stone

facts.quizQuestion: Pteranodon was a flying reptile — what group does it belong to?
facts.explanation: Pteranodon was a pterosaur, not a dinosaur. Pterosaurs were a distinct lineage of flying reptiles that lived alongside dinosaurs but are not classified within Dinosauria. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
facts.visualDescription: A large winged reptile soaring over Cretaceous seas, crested head sweeping back, leathery wings spread wide against a pale sky

facts.quizQuestion: Pteranodon had a wingspan of approximately how many meters?
facts.explanation: Pteranodon had a wingspan of about 6 to 7 meters, making it one of the largest flying animals of the Cretaceous. It soared over the Western Interior Seaway using thermals like modern sea birds. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
facts.visualDescription: Top-down silhouette of a Pteranodon with outstretched wings, length measurement lines showing its impressive 6.5-meter span

facts.quizQuestion: Which Cretaceous pterosaur had a distinctive backward-pointing head crest and was completely toothless?
facts.explanation: Pteranodon's name literally means 'winged and toothless'. It had a prominent backward-pointing crest and caught fish over the Western Interior Seaway that covered much of North America. Source: Wikipedia/Pteranodon
facts.visualDescription: A crested pterosaur skimming ocean waves, beak dipping toward the water to snatch fish, elongated crest streaming behind its head

facts.quizQuestion: Which pterosaur holds the record as the largest flying animal ever known, with a wingspan of about 10.5 meters?
facts.explanation: Quetzalcoatlus is the largest known flying animal in Earth's history, with a wingspan of roughly 10 to 11 meters. When standing on the ground it was giraffe-sized — about 5 meters tall. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
facts.visualDescription: An enormous pterosaur standing upright among trees, its head level with the canopy, tiny humans shown beside it for scale

facts.quizQuestion: The giant pterosaur Quetzalcoatlus was named after which Aztec deity?
facts.explanation: Quetzalcoatlus takes its name from Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god of Aztec mythology. The pterosaur was discovered in Texas (Hell Creek Formation) and named in 1975 by Douglas Lawson. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
facts.visualDescription: An ancient Aztec stone carving of the feathered serpent deity beside a skeletal reconstruction of the giant pterosaur named after it

facts.quizQuestion: When standing on the ground, Quetzalcoatlus was roughly the same height as which modern animal?
facts.explanation: Quetzalcoatlus stood approximately 5 meters tall when on the ground — roughly the height of a giraffe. Despite being a flier, it likely stalked prey on land, similar to a modern stork. Source: Wikipedia/Quetzalcoatlus
facts.visualDescription: A Quetzalcoatlus walking on land beside a giraffe, both reaching the same height, dwarfing humans nearby

facts.quizQuestion: Which Early Jurassic pterosaur is named for having two distinct types of teeth — unusual among pterosaurs?
facts.explanation: Dimorphodon's name means 'two-form tooth', reflecting its unique dentition with two different tooth shapes. It was found on the Jurassic Coast of Dorset by Mary Anning and described by Richard Owen in 1859. Source: Wikipedia/Dimorphodon
facts.visualDescription: A small pterosaur with a disproportionately large head and short wings clambering along coastal cliffs, its mixed dentition visible in the open beak

facts.quizQuestion: The Dimorphodon pterosaur fossil was discovered on the Jurassic Coast of Dorset by which pioneering fossil hunter?
facts.explanation: Mary Anning found the Dimorphodon fossil in 1828 on the Jurassic Coast near Lyme Regis, Dorset. It was the first pterosaur specimen found outside Germany. Anning also discovered the first complete Ichthyosaurus and the first Plesiosaurus. Source: Wikipedia/Dimorphodon
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian-era woman fossil hunter on rugged coastal cliffs, examining a winged reptile fossil embedded in limestone

facts.quizQuestion: Mosasaurus was a giant marine reptile more closely related to which group of modern animals than to dinosaurs?
facts.explanation: Mosasaurus was not a dinosaur or plesiosaur — it was a mosasaurid, part of a lineage most closely related to modern monitor lizards (Varanidae). It had a double-hinged jaw similar to snakes for swallowing large prey. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
facts.visualDescription: A massive serpentine sea creature with four paddle-like limbs surging through dark ocean water, its lizard-like head agape with rows of conical teeth

facts.quizQuestion: The Mosasaurus was first discovered near which city in the Netherlands — a name reflected in its meaning 'Meuse lizard'?
facts.explanation: The first Mosasaurus specimens were found near Maastricht in the Netherlands, near the Meuse River (Mosa in Latin), giving the animal its name. It was one of the most fearsome apex predators of the Late Cretaceous seas. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
facts.visualDescription: A fossil skull of a giant marine reptile displayed in a museum case, a map inset showing Maastricht near the Meuse River in the Netherlands

facts.quizQuestion: Which giant marine reptile was the apex ocean predator of the Late Cretaceous, famously featured in Jurassic World?
facts.explanation: Mosasaurus was the dominant marine predator of the Late Cretaceous oceans. Up to 13 meters long and 14 tonnes, it could consume virtually any prey. It featured prominently in the Jurassic World film franchise. Source: Wikipedia/Mosasaurus
facts.visualDescription: An enormous marine predator breaching the ocean surface to seize prey in its massive jaws, Late Cretaceous seas around it

facts.quizQuestion: Who found the first complete Ichthyosaurus skeleton near Lyme Regis, England, around 1811 at approximately age 12?
facts.explanation: Mary Anning discovered the first complete Ichthyosaurus skeleton near Lyme Regis around 1811 when she was about 12 years old. This landmark find launched her career as one of history's greatest fossil hunters. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A young Victorian girl and her brother on a rocky beach, uncovering an extraordinary dolphin-shaped fossil skeleton from the cliffs of Lyme Regis

facts.quizQuestion: Which dolphin-shaped marine reptile evolved a streamlined body through convergent evolution — looking like a fish but being a reptile?
facts.explanation: Ichthyosaurus evolved a dolphin-like body shape independently of dolphins — a classic example of convergent evolution. Unlike fish, it breathed air and gave birth to live young. Fossils with embryos inside have been discovered. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A sleek dolphin-shaped marine reptile cutting through Jurassic seas, its large eye adapted for deep diving, small embryo visible in a comparative diagram

facts.quizQuestion: Unlike many reptiles, Ichthyosaurus gave birth how — as proven by fossils showing mothers with embryos?
facts.explanation: Exceptional fossils of Ichthyosaurus have been found with embryos preserved inside the mother's body, proving they gave birth to live young rather than laying eggs — an adaptation to their fully aquatic lifestyle. Source: Wikipedia/Ichthyosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A museum-quality fossil slab showing an Ichthyosaurus skeleton with several small embryo skeletons preserved inside the rib cage

facts.quizQuestion: Which long-necked marine reptile with four paddle-like flippers is often cited as the inspiration for the Loch Ness Monster myth?
facts.explanation: Plesiosaurus, with its long neck, small head, and four flippers, inspired the popular image of the Loch Ness Monster. In reality, plesiosaurs were ocean-dwellers and could not raise their long necks far above the water surface. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A graceful long-necked marine reptile gliding underwater, four large paddles propelling it, its small head emerging just above the surface in misty waters

facts.quizQuestion: In what year did Mary Anning discover the first complete Plesiosaurus fossil at Lyme Regis?
facts.explanation: Mary Anning found the first complete Plesiosaurus at Lyme Regis in 1823. The specimen was described by William Conybeare and was so unusual that the Geological Society initially questioned its authenticity. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A beautifully preserved fossil skeleton of a long-necked plesiosaur laid out on a rock surface, the distinctive paddle-like limbs and long neck clearly visible

facts.quizQuestion: True or false: Plesiosaurus could raise its long neck high above the water surface like a swan.
facts.explanation: False — despite popular depictions, plesiosaurs could not raise their necks high above water. The vertebral structure prevented swan-like poses. The popular image of the Loch Ness Monster with its neck raised is anatomically impossible for a plesiosaur. Source: Wikipedia/Plesiosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A side-by-side comparison: a popular (incorrect) depiction of a plesiosaur with neck raised high vs. a scientifically accurate view of the neck held mostly horizontal near the water

facts.quizQuestion: Which 150-million-year-old fossil is the most famous transitional link between non-avian dinosaurs and modern birds?
facts.explanation: Archaeopteryx, found in the Solnhofen limestone of Bavaria in 1861, had feathered wings like a bird but also retained dinosaurian features: teeth, clawed fingers on its wings, and a long bony tail. It remains the most celebrated transitional fossil in paleontology. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
facts.visualDescription: A beautifully preserved fossil in pale limestone showing the outline of feathered wings, teeth, and a long bony tail — the perfect blend of bird and dinosaur

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was the first Archaeopteryx specimen found — just two years after Darwin published On the Origin of Species?
facts.explanation: The first Archaeopteryx specimen was discovered in 1861, only two years after Darwin's On the Origin of Species (1859). Its intermediate features between dinosaurs and birds provided powerful support for Darwin's theory at exactly the right moment. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
facts.visualDescription: A timeline comparing Darwin's book publication in 1859 with the Archaeopteryx discovery in 1861, a fossil slab shown alongside Darwin's famous portrait

facts.quizQuestion: Although Archaeopteryx had feathered wings like a bird, it still possessed which distinctly un-bird-like feature?
facts.explanation: Archaeopteryx retained teeth in its beak, along with clawed fingers on its wings and a long bony tail — all dinosaurian features. Modern birds have none of these, making Archaeopteryx a crucial transitional specimen. Source: Wikipedia/Archaeopteryx
facts.visualDescription: A close-up reconstruction of an Archaeopteryx head showing its feathered body combined with a toothed jaw, unlike any modern bird

facts.quizQuestion: Which small omnivorous dinosaur from Argentina, dating to 231 million years ago, is one of the earliest known dinosaurs — its name meaning 'dawn thief'?
facts.explanation: Eoraptor, meaning 'dawn thief', lived approximately 231 million years ago during the Late Triassic. It was discovered in the Ischigualasto Formation of Argentina by Paul Sereno in 1991 and provides key insight into the earliest dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
facts.visualDescription: A small, lightly built bipedal dinosaur darting through a Triassic landscape of sparse ferns and conifer trees, tiny and fast compared to later giants

facts.quizQuestion: Eoraptor was discovered in 1991 in Argentina's Ischigualasto Formation by which American paleontologist?
facts.explanation: Paul Sereno and his team discovered Eoraptor in 1991 in the Ischigualasto Formation, also known as the Valle de la Luna, in the San Juan Province of Argentina. Sereno is known for discovering some of the oldest and largest dinosaurs. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
facts.visualDescription: A paleontologist carefully brushing rock away from a small dinosaur skeleton in an arid Argentine desert landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Eoraptor, one of the earliest known dinosaurs, lived during which geological period approximately 231 million years ago?
facts.explanation: Eoraptor lived during the Late Triassic period, approximately 231 million years ago. Dinosaurs first appeared during the Triassic, shortly after the catastrophic Permian-Triassic mass extinction wiped out most of Earth's species. Source: Wikipedia/Eoraptor
facts.visualDescription: A geological timeline showing the Triassic period highlighted, with a small Eoraptor silhouette placed at 231 million years ago

facts.quizQuestion: The Triassic period lasted from approximately how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
facts.explanation: The Triassic period spanned from approximately 252 to 201 million years ago. It began after the devastating Permian-Triassic mass extinction (the 'Great Dying') and ended with another extinction event that opened the door for dinosaurs to dominate. Source: Wikipedia/Triassic
facts.visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Triassic band highlighted in warm red-orange, spanning from 252 to 201 million years ago

facts.quizQuestion: During the Triassic period, the first dinosaurs appeared approximately how many million years ago?
facts.explanation: The earliest known dinosaurs, including Eoraptor and Herrerasaurus, appeared approximately 231 million years ago in the Late Triassic. They arose in what is now Argentina, shortly after the Permian-Triassic mass extinction had cleared ecological niches. Source: Wikipedia/Triassic
facts.visualDescription: A Late Triassic landscape with small early dinosaurs running among larger non-dinosaur reptiles, the southern supercontinent Gondwana stretching to the horizon

facts.quizQuestion: During the Triassic period, all continents were joined in which single supercontinent?
facts.explanation: During the Triassic, Earth's landmasses were merged into the supercontinent Pangaea. The hot, dry climate created by this vast landmass shaped the environments where the first dinosaurs evolved. Pangaea began breaking apart in the Early Jurassic (~175 mya). Source: Wikipedia/Pangaea
facts.visualDescription: A globe showing all continents fused together into the supercontinent Pangaea, surrounded by the vast Panthalassa ocean, during the Triassic period

facts.quizQuestion: The Jurassic period spanned approximately from how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
facts.explanation: The Jurassic period lasted from approximately 201 to 145 million years ago. It saw the dominance of sauropods as the largest land animals, the evolution of the first birds from theropod dinosaurs, and the break-up of Pangaea. Source: Wikipedia/Jurassic
facts.visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Jurassic band highlighted in green, spanning from 201 to 145 million years ago

facts.quizQuestion: During the Late Jurassic (~150 mya), which famous transitional fossil marks the evolution of birds from theropod dinosaurs?
facts.explanation: Archaeopteryx, dating to approximately 150 million years ago in the Late Jurassic, is the most famous evidence that birds evolved from theropod dinosaurs. Its discovery in 1861 provided crucial support for Darwin's theory of evolution published just two years earlier. Source: Wikipedia/Jurassic
facts.visualDescription: A fossil slab of Archaeopteryx in limestone beside an illustration of the living animal — a feathered creature mid-way between dinosaur and bird

facts.quizQuestion: The Cretaceous period lasted from approximately how many million years ago to how many million years ago?
facts.explanation: The Cretaceous period spanned from approximately 145 to 66 million years ago — the longest period of the Mesozoic Era. It ended with the K-Pg mass extinction event caused by the Chicxulub asteroid impact. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous
facts.visualDescription: A geological timescale chart with the Cretaceous band highlighted in teal-blue, spanning from 145 to 66 million years ago with a dramatic impact marker at the end

facts.quizQuestion: The asteroid that caused the K-Pg mass extinction 66 million years ago was estimated to be approximately how many kilometers in diameter?
facts.explanation: The Chicxulub asteroid that struck Earth 66 million years ago is estimated to have been 10 to 15 kilometers in diameter. The impact released energy equivalent to billions of nuclear bombs, triggering wildfires, a dust cloud blocking sunlight, and a global 'impact winter'. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
facts.visualDescription: A massive glowing asteroid trailing fire plunging toward a Cretaceous Earth, dinosaurs visible on the ground below as the sky ignites

facts.quizQuestion: The Chicxulub crater, formed by the asteroid that wiped out the non-avian dinosaurs, is located beneath which region?
facts.explanation: The Chicxulub impact crater lies beneath the Yucatan Peninsula of Mexico and the Gulf of Mexico. About 180 kilometers wide, it is one of the largest confirmed impact structures on Earth. The impact 66 million years ago caused the K-Pg mass extinction. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
facts.visualDescription: A satellite map showing the circular outline of the Chicxulub crater partially offshore the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico, with an impact illustration overlay

facts.quizQuestion: The K-Pg extinction event 66 million years ago wiped out approximately what percentage of all species on Earth?
facts.explanation: The Cretaceous-Paleogene (K-Pg) extinction event eliminated approximately 75% of all species on Earth. Non-avian dinosaurs were entirely wiped out, while birds (avian dinosaurs), mammals, crocodilians, and some other groups survived. Source: Wikipedia/Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction event
facts.visualDescription: A dramatic infographic showing 75% of animal silhouettes grayed out and crossed, with surviving groups — birds, mammals, crocodiles — highlighted in color

facts.quizQuestion: Which British scientist coined the term 'Dinosauria' in 1842, meaning 'terrible lizards'?
facts.explanation: Richard Owen coined the word 'Dinosauria' in 1842 to group together Megalosaurus, Iguanodon, and Hylaeosaurus as a distinct class of ancient reptiles. He also founded the Natural History Museum in London. Source: Wikipedia/Richard Owen
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian-era portrait of a distinguished scientist beside a reconstruction of a large dinosaur skeleton, the word 'Dinosauria' written in ornate 19th-century script

facts.quizQuestion: Richard Owen, who coined the term 'Dinosauria', founded which famous museum in London?
facts.explanation: Richard Owen was instrumental in founding the Natural History Museum in London, which opened in 1881. He believed natural history collections should be accessible to the public, not just scientists. Source: Wikipedia/Richard Owen
facts.visualDescription: The iconic Romanesque facade of London's Natural History Museum, with a giant dinosaur skeleton displayed prominently in the entrance hall

facts.quizQuestion: Which pioneering 19th-century fossil hunter from Lyme Regis discovered the first Ichthyosaurus, first Plesiosaurus, and first pterosaur outside Germany?
facts.explanation: Mary Anning (1799–1847) made world-changing fossil discoveries along the Jurassic Coast of Dorset: the first complete Ichthyosaurus (~1811), the first Plesiosaurus (1823), and the Dimorphodon pterosaur (1828). She was largely uncredited in her lifetime due to gender and social class. Source: Wikipedia/Mary Anning
facts.visualDescription: A determined young woman in Victorian dress walking the fossil-strewn cliffs of Lyme Regis with a hammer, loyal dog by her side, a stormy grey sea behind her

facts.quizQuestion: Which pioneering fossil hunter is popularly believed to have inspired the tongue twister 'She sells sea shells by the sea shore'?
facts.explanation: Mary Anning sold fossils to support her family on the beaches of Lyme Regis, and many believe she inspired the famous tongue twister 'She sells sea shells by the sea shore'. Although the connection is disputed, it reflects how well-known she was in her era. Source: Wikipedia/Mary Anning
facts.visualDescription: A Victorian seaside scene with a woman selling fossils from a basket on a pebble beach, tourists examining ammonites, belemnites, and ichthyosaur bones

facts.quizQuestion: The 'Bone Wars' (1877–1892) were a fierce rivalry between Edward Drinker Cope and which other American paleontologist?
facts.explanation: The Bone Wars pitted Edward Drinker Cope against Othniel Charles Marsh in a bitter race to discover and name the most dinosaur species. Together they described 142 new species using methods that included espionage, bribery, and sabotage. Source: Wikipedia/Bone Wars
facts.visualDescription: Two mustachioed Victorian-era scientists glaring at each other across a map of the American West dotted with fossil excavation sites, shovel in each man's hand

facts.quizQuestion: Marsh and Cope together discovered how many new dinosaur species during the Bone Wars?
facts.explanation: Between them, Othniel Charles Marsh and Edward Drinker Cope described approximately 142 new dinosaur species during the Bone Wars (1877–1892). Marsh named Apatosaurus, Brontosaurus, Stegosaurus, and Triceratops; Cope named Camarasaurus, Coelophysis, and many others. Source: Wikipedia/Bone Wars
facts.visualDescription: A ledger-style infographic listing the dinosaur names discovered by Marsh and Cope, with a tally counter showing 142 species total

facts.quizQuestion: Which dinosaur was the first to be formally named in science, described by William Buckland in 1824?
facts.explanation: Megalosaurus, meaning 'great lizard', was formally named in 1824 by William Buckland — making it the first dinosaur to receive a scientific name. Iguanodon followed in 1825. Neither species had been identified as a 'dinosaur' yet, as Richard Owen didn't coin that term until 1842. Source: Wikipedia/Megalosaurus
facts.visualDescription: A 19th-century engraving of Megalosaurus as scientists imagined it — a hulking quadrupedal beast — beside the modern bipedal reconstruction

facts.quizQuestion: Pangaea, the supercontinent that existed during the Triassic, began breaking apart during which geological period approximately 175 million years ago?
facts.explanation: Pangaea began breaking apart in the Early Jurassic, approximately 175 million years ago. The separation created the proto-Atlantic Ocean and split the landmass into Laurasia (north) and Gondwana (south), eventually leading to the modern continents. Source: Wikipedia/Pangaea
facts.visualDescription: A sequence of four globe maps showing Pangaea intact in the Triassic, beginning to rift in the Jurassic, splitting into Laurasia and Gondwana, and approaching modern continental positions

facts.quizQuestion: Where were the feathered dinosaur fossils discovered in the 1990s that revolutionized our understanding of dinosaur evolution?
facts.explanation: Extraordinary feathered dinosaur fossils were discovered in Liaoning Province, China, starting in the 1990s. These included Sinosauropteryx (confirmed 1996) and many others, proving that feathers were widespread among theropod dinosaurs long before the evolution of birds. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
facts.visualDescription: A Chinese paleontologist examining a beautifully preserved fossil showing clear feather impressions on a small theropod dinosaur in fine-grained shale

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was Sinosauropteryx confirmed as the first feathered non-avian dinosaur, revolutionizing paleontology?
facts.explanation: Sinosauropteryx was confirmed in 1996 as the first feathered non-avian dinosaur. Found in Liaoning Province, China, its discovery was part of a wave of Chinese feathered fossil finds that proved feathers evolved in theropod dinosaurs before the origin of birds. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
facts.visualDescription: A milestone timeline in paleontology history, with 1996 marked for the Sinosauropteryx confirmation beside an illustration of the small feathered dinosaur

facts.quizQuestion: In what year was physical proof discovered that Velociraptor had feathers — in the form of quill knobs on its arm bones?
facts.explanation: In 2007, scientists confirmed that Velociraptor had feathers, discovering quill knobs (feather anchor points) on the ulna bone of its forearm. This matched similar structures found in modern birds and confirmed that the famous predator was feathered, not scaly. Source: Wikipedia/Feathered dinosaur
facts.visualDescription: A diagram of a Velociraptor forearm bone with quill knobs highlighted, compared to a modern eagle wing bone showing the same anchoring structures

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Albertosaurus, a tyrannosaurid from Late Cretaceous Alberta, Canada. Smaller than T. rex but with a similar build, Albertosaurus was a fast predator whose fossils include a remarkable bonebed of 26 individuals found by Barnum Brown in 1910.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a medium-sized bipedal theropod with a long narrow skull and small arms, similar in shape to T. rex but more slender

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Allosaurus, the apex predator of the Late Jurassic period. Up to 8.5 meters long, it dominated the Morrison Formation of North America alongside sauropods like Diplodocus and Brachiosaurus.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large Jurassic theropod with strong three-clawed arms, a muscular frame, and a broad skull with prominent brow ridges

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Ankylosaurus, the heavily armored herbivore of the Late Cretaceous. Its entire back was covered in bony plates called osteoderms, and its massive tail club could shatter predator bones.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a low, wide, four-legged dinosaur covered in bony armor plates across its back and sides, with a large bony club at the end of its tail

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Apatosaurus (also known as Brontosaurus — both names are now scientifically valid). This massive sauropod had a very long neck and whip-like tail and lived during the Late Jurassic in North America.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive long-necked sauropod dinosaur with a thick body, very long horizontal neck, and a tapering whip-like tail

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Archaeopteryx, the famous transitional fossil between non-avian dinosaurs and birds. It had feathered wings like a bird, but also retained teeth, clawed fingers, and a long bony tail like a dinosaur.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a small feathered creature with toothed beak, clawed wing tips, and a long tail — unmistakably between a dinosaur and a bird

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Argentinosaurus, one of the largest land animals ever to live. Estimated at up to 35 meters long and 70 tonnes, this titanosaur sauropod from Patagonia, Argentina lived during the Late Cretaceous.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a colossal sauropod dinosaur dwarfing everything around it, its immense column-like legs supporting a massive barrel-shaped body and very long neck

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Baryonyx, a fish-eating spinosaurid from Early Cretaceous England. It had a long crocodile-like skull and a massive curved thumb claw — its name means 'heavy claw'. It was discovered in Surrey, England in 1983.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large theropod with a notably elongated crocodile-like skull, wading at the edge of a river, one enormous hooked claw prominently visible on its hand

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Brachiosaurus, a Late Jurassic sauropod famous for having its front legs longer than its hind legs — giving it a giraffe-like upward-reaching posture. It could reach vegetation high in the tree canopy.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a sauropod with noticeably longer front legs than hind legs, holding its neck in a giraffe-like upright posture, browsing high in the treetops

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
