facts.explanation: This is Carcharodontosaurus, the 'shark-toothed lizard' from Late Cretaceous North Africa. One of the largest carnivorous dinosaurs ever, it coexisted with Spinosaurus in the ancient rivers and deltas of what is now Morocco and Egypt.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive bipedal carnivore with a large skull, serrated shark-like teeth, and powerful build, set in a North African Cretaceous environment

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Carnotaurus, the 'meat-eating bull' from Late Cretaceous Argentina. It is uniquely identified by the two thick bull-like horns above its eyes — the only large theropod known to have horns.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a bipedal theropod dinosaur with two distinctive bull-like horns jutting above its eyes, very reduced vestigial arms, and a muscular compact body

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Ceratosaurus, a Late Jurassic predator from the Morrison Formation. Its most distinctive feature is the single horn on its nose — giving it the name 'horned lizard'. It also had a row of bony osteoderms running down its back.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a medium-sized Jurassic theropod with a prominent single horn on its nose, a row of bony ridges along its back, and a relatively large head

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Coelophysis, one of the earliest known dinosaurs from the Late Triassic period. A slender, fast predator from Ghost Ranch, New Mexico, hundreds of its skeletons were found together in one of the most spectacular dinosaur bonebeds ever discovered.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a very slender, lightly built early theropod with a long narrow head, long neck, and gracile limbs — distinctly early Triassic in character

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Compsognathus, one of the smallest known non-avian dinosaurs at roughly 65 centimeters long — about the size of a chicken. It lived in Late Jurassic Europe and was found in the same Solnhofen limestone as Archaeopteryx.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a very small theropod dinosaur — roughly chicken-sized — with a long neck, small arms, and an agile build, running across coastal mudflats

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Corythosaurus, a Late Cretaceous hadrosaur whose name means 'helmet lizard'. Its tall, rounded head crest functioned as a resonating chamber for producing distinctive low-frequency calls for communication within the herd.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large duck-billed hadrosaur with a distinctive tall rounded helmet-shaped crest on top of its head

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Deinonychus, the 'terrible claw'. At 3.4 meters long, it was the real inspiration for the Jurassic Park 'Velociraptors' — which were actually based on Deinonychus, not the turkey-sized true Velociraptor.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a feathered dromaeosaurid — about the size of a large turkey or wolf — with a distinctive raised sickle claw on each hind foot, in a dynamic stalking pose

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Dilophosaurus, the 'two-crested lizard' from Early Jurassic Arizona. It is identified by the distinctive pair of thin parallel crests running along its skull. Despite what Jurassic Park shows, it had no frill and no venom.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a slender Early Jurassic theropod with two thin parallel bony crests running side-by-side along the top of its long skull

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Diplodocus, one of the longest dinosaurs at up to 26 meters. This Late Jurassic sauropod had a pencil-like neck, peg-like teeth for stripping leaves, and a whip-like tail. Casts of its skeleton were distributed to museums worldwide.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a very long sauropod with a thin whip-like tail, relatively slender neck held nearly horizontally, and pencil-like teeth, walking across an open Jurassic floodplain

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Edmontosaurus, a large duck-billed hadrosaur from the very end of the Cretaceous. One of the last non-avian dinosaurs, it survived right up to the K-Pg extinction. Mummified specimens with preserved skin have been discovered.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large duck-billed hadrosaur without a pronounced head crest, bulky and quadrupedal, with a broad flat bill and thick skin impressions visible

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Eoraptor, one of the earliest known dinosaurs from 231 million years ago in Argentina. Only about 1.7 meters long, this small omnivore shows the primitive features that all dinosaurs descended from.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a small, lightly built bipedal dinosaur in an open Triassic landscape, about the size of a large dog, with slender limbs and a small toothed head

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Gallimimus, an ornithomimid dinosaur whose name means 'chicken mimic'. One of the fastest dinosaurs, it had a toothless beak, long neck, and ostrich-like proportions. A herd of Gallimimus was famously featured in Jurassic Park.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a long-legged, ostrich-like dinosaur with a toothless beak, long neck, and slender body built for speed, running across a Mongolian Cretaceous landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Giganotosaurus, a massive predator from Patagonian Argentina that rivals T. rex in size at about 12.5 meters long. It hunted giant sauropods and had a long, slim skull with serrated blade-like teeth.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive South American theropod with an elongated skull and powerful build, set in a Cretaceous Patagonian landscape with giant sauropods in the background

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine reptile is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Ichthyosaurus, a dolphin-shaped marine reptile from the Early Jurassic. Despite looking like a dolphin, it is a reptile — an example of convergent evolution. Its fossils, including mothers with embryos, were famously discovered by Mary Anning.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a streamlined dolphin-shaped marine reptile with large eyes, a vertical crescent tail fin, and dorsal fin, cutting through Jurassic seas

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Iguanodon, the second dinosaur ever formally named (1825). It is distinctive for its thumb spike — originally mistakenly placed on its nose. A large herbivore of the Early Cretaceous, 29 articulated skeletons were found in a Belgian mine in 1878.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large robust herbivorous dinosaur walking on two or four legs, with a distinctive spike thumb visible on its front hands

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Maiasaura, the 'good mother lizard'. Jack Horner's 1978 discovery of its nesting colonies in Montana proved dinosaurs cared for their young — overturning the image of dinosaurs as neglectful parents.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a hadrosaur near a nest of eggs, with small hatchlings around it, the parent dinosaur attentively tending to its young

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine predator is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Mosasaurus, the dominant apex predator of the Late Cretaceous seas. Up to 13 meters long, this giant marine lizard (related to modern monitor lizards) had a double-hinged jaw and ruled the oceans until the K-Pg extinction.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive elongated marine predator with four paddle-like limbs, a long jaw full of conical teeth, and a powerful laterally flattened tail

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Oviraptor, a small feathered omnivore from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. Its name means 'egg thief' — it was wrongly accused of stealing Protoceratops eggs. Later discoveries proved it was actually brooding its own eggs, much like a bird.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a small feathered omnivore with a toothless parrot-like beak, prominent head crest, and feathered arms, posed over a nest of eggs

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Pachycephalosaurus, the 'thick-headed lizard'. Its domed skull was up to 25 centimeters thick and may have been used in head-butting contests or flank displays to establish dominance.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a bipedal herbivore with a massively thick rounded dome on top of its head, small bony knobs and spikes around the edges of the dome

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Parasaurolophus, instantly recognized by its long curved hollow crest sweeping back from the skull. CT scans confirmed the crest worked as a resonating chamber for producing trumpeting low-frequency calls.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large hadrosaur with a dramatic long curved crest sweeping backward from the top of its skull, highlighted against a Cretaceous forest background

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric marine reptile is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Plesiosaurus, a long-necked marine reptile of the Early Jurassic. With its four large paddle flippers and long neck, it inspired popular images of the Loch Ness Monster. It was first discovered by Mary Anning in 1823.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a marine reptile with a very long thin neck, small head, barrel-shaped body, and four large paddle-like flippers, swimming gracefully through Jurassic seas

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Protoceratops, a small early ceratopsian from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. It lacked the large developed horns of its relatives, but had a distinctive neck frill. Famous for the 'fighting dinosaurs' fossil showing it locked in combat with a Velociraptor.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a small four-legged herbivore with a prominent bony neck frill, a parrot-like beak, and no large facial horns, from Late Cretaceous Mongolia

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric flying reptile is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Pteranodon, a large Cretaceous pterosaur (flying reptile, not a dinosaur) with a distinctive backward-pointing head crest and a toothless beak. With a wingspan of about 6.5 meters, it soared over the ancient seas of North America.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large pterosaur with a prominent backward-pointing crest, long toothless beak, and leathery wings spread wide in flight over a coastal Cretaceous landscape

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric flying reptile is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Quetzalcoatlus (or a close relative), the largest flying animal ever known. With a wingspan of up to 10.5 meters, it was giraffe-sized when standing. Named after the Aztec feathered serpent god.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a truly massive pterosaur with an enormous wingspan, long toothless beak, and giraffe-like proportions when standing on all four limbs on the ground

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Spinosaurus, the largest carnivorous dinosaur ever discovered at about 14 meters long. Identified by its spectacular sail of neural spines up to 1.65 meters tall, it was a semi-aquatic fish-eater with a crocodile-like snout.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive theropod with a dramatic sail of tall neural spines running along its back, a long crocodilian snout, and powerful arms, wading in a river

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Stegosaurus, the iconic Late Jurassic herbivore recognized by its double row of large bony plates along its back and four tail spikes (the 'thagomizer'). Despite its large body, it had a relatively small brain.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a large four-legged herbivore with two staggered rows of tall diamond-shaped bony plates along its back and four pointed spikes at the end of its tail

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Styracosaurus, the 'spiked lizard' from Late Cretaceous Alberta. Distinguished by its spectacular frill adorned with multiple long spikes radiating outward, plus a single large nasal horn — more elaborate than its relative Triceratops.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a ceratopsian dinosaur with a spectacular frill edged by multiple long radiating spikes and a prominent single nose horn — a more ornate version of Triceratops

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Therizinosaurus, the 'scythe lizard'. Despite being a theropod (the same group as T. rex), it was a herbivore with the longest claws of any known animal — up to one meter long. A bizarre and unique dinosaur from Late Cretaceous Mongolia.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a bizarre theropod dinosaur with an enormous potbelly, feathered body, and impossibly long curved claws — up to a meter each — used for pulling down vegetation

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Triceratops, one of the most recognizable dinosaurs. With three facial horns and a large bony neck frill, it was a contemporary of T. rex in the Late Cretaceous. Evidence of combat between the two species has been found on fossilized bones.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive four-legged herbivore with three facial horns (two long brow horns and one shorter nose horn) and a large solid bony neck frill

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Tyrannosaurus rex, the iconic apex predator of the Late Cretaceous. Up to 12 meters long and 8.4 tonnes, it had banana-sized teeth, binocular vision, and tiny vestigial forelimbs. The Sue specimen at the Field Museum in Chicago is the most complete T. rex ever found.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a massive bipedal predator with tiny arms and an enormous head filled with large teeth — the archetypal Tyrannosaurus rex pose

facts.quizQuestion: Which prehistoric creature is shown in this illustration?
facts.explanation: This is Velociraptor, a feathered dromaeosaurid from Late Cretaceous Mongolia. Only turkey-sized in reality (not the large Jurassic Park version), it had a sickle-shaped killing claw on each foot and feathers confirmed by quill knobs found on its arm bones in 2007.
facts.visualDescription: Illustration of a feathered dromaeosaurid roughly the size of a turkey, with a distinctive raised sickle claw on the second toe of each foot, running across a Mongolian Cretaceous landscape
